creator: pmc@citylink.dinoex.sub.org
text:
(In reply to Christian Ullrich from comment #6)
I agree, using Kerberos for SSO is where the real fun is, and
postgres nicely supports that.

Then also, I did some investigations, as follows:

1.
postgres-16 seems to compile just fine if only you tune a few
variables in /etc/make.conf

2.
Alternatively, it took me two hours to do what the original Frostism
deliberately neglects to offer: patch the new credstore-extensions
out of the postgres-16 code and make it behave like postgres-15 in
regard to kerberos.

3.
The problem with the "ancient Heimdal in base that you should not
concern" is that this is the kerberos that ssh compiles with, and
NFSv4 probably also. So for now, it might be a bit difficult to
not concern.

4.
My opinion and recommentation up to now was to not run more than
one kerberos per system, so if need is to use MIT, then compile
base WITHOUT_KERBEROS.

Now I did some further tests, and it seems that Heimdal-in-base and
MIT-from-ports can be installed together at the same time.
Heimdal-in-base will continue to use /etc/krb5.conf and
/etc/krb5.keytab, and the ssh will use this one.

All ports that desire to use MIT can link against MIT-from-ports,
and this will be installed as a prerequisite port. That
configuration can then be placed into /usr/local/etc/krb5.conf and
/usr/local/etc/krb5.keytab, and the respective ports can be
started from /etc/rc.conf like so:
  pgadmin4_env="KRB5_CONFIG=/usr/local/etc/krb5.conf"
  openssh_env="KRB5_KTNAME=/usr/local/etc/krb5.keytab"

Sure, adminstration then is a bit of a pain, because you have to
take good care about which tool in which path you run for what.
But, this is not for eternity, as there is work underway to update
the Heimdal-in-base by some recent MIT version.
